metadata.title: Why Every Qubit Lives on a Globe
metadata.color_semantics: TEAL = state arrow (the qubit's Bloch vector); CRIMSON = analyzer arrow (the measurement direction); the angle between them determines the probability
metadata.note: Exclusions honored: no half-angle proof from the amplitudes, no gate operations, no density-matrix/mixed states.
_variant_todo: rewrite every beat narration_text in the Teardown register (voices/teardown/VOICE.md + CLAUDE-BRAND.md) — voice only, facts unchanged
signature tangent 0-1 per video, ONLY on a clear opportunity (see SKILL)
swap the outro to the Claude outro from AUTHOR.MD :: NikBearBrown
then build audience-namespaced (audio in the new voice, scenes in the new palette)

skin_warnings: B01: palette=claude but the cold open is 'un-annotated' — COLD OPEN LAW wants ClaudeComposerAsk
B13: palette=claude but the outro is 'OutroCTA' — OUTRO LAW wants ClaudeTitleOutro

beats.narration_text: This is Liam, in for Bear. Every possible state of a quantum bit is a single point on one sphere — and the chance any measurement gives 'up' is just how far you've tilted from the top.

card.copy: Why Every Qubit Lives on a Globe

build.suggested: suggested prompt: This is Liam, in for Bear. Every possible state of a quantum bit is a single point on one sphere — and the chance any measurement gives 'up' is just how far you

beats.narration_text: A qubit is a quantum system with two possible outcomes: zero and one, up and down. Its full state is a superposition: alpha times zero plus beta times one, where both are complex numbers. Quantum computers manipulate thousands of these simultaneously.

build.suggested: suggested prompt: A qubit is a quantum system with two possible outcomes: zero and one, up and down. Its full state is a superposition: alpha times zero plus beta times one, wher

beats.narration_text: The Born rule says the probability of measuring zero is the squared modulus of alpha: a number between zero and one. For any measurement result the probabilities must sum to one. So the two complex numbers are tightly constrained.

build.suggested: suggested prompt: The Born rule says the probability of measuring zero is the squared modulus of alpha: a number between zero and one. For any measurement result the probabilitie

beats.narration_text: The Question: a qubit's state has two complex numbers and seems to need four real parameters to describe it. Yet every qubit maps to a single point on a sphere, which takes only two. Where do the extra parameters go, and why a sphere?

card.copy: A qubit needs four real parameters — yet it maps to two. Where do the extras go, and why a sphere?

build.suggested: suggested prompt: The Question: a qubit's state has two complex numbers and seems to need four real parameters to describe it. Yet every qubit maps to a single point on a sphere,

beats.narration_text: Two complex numbers should need four real parameters to fully specify. That suggests the space of qubit states is four-dimensional — a large, complicated space, not anything like a simple surface. A sphere is two-dimensional, so the map seems to throw away information.

build.suggested: suggested prompt: Two complex numbers should need four real parameters to fully specify. That suggests the space of qubit states is four-dimensional — a large, complicated space,

beats.narration_text: Constraint one: normalization. The probabilities must sum to one, so the squared moduli of the two amplitudes add to one. That removes one degree of freedom — from four real parameters down to three.

build.suggested: suggested prompt: Constraint one: normalization. The probabilities must sum to one, so the squared moduli of the two amplitudes add to one. That removes one degree of freedom — f

beats.narration_text: Constraint two: global phase. If you multiply the entire state by any complex number of magnitude one — any phase — every probability you can measure is unchanged. That removes one more parameter. Three minus one equals two. And two real parameters label points on a sphere.

build.suggested: suggested prompt: Constraint two: global phase. If you multiply the entire state by any complex number of magnitude one — any phase — every probability you can measure is unchang

beats.narration_text: The two surviving parameters become a polar angle theta and an azimuth phi. Theta controls how much up versus how much down — at the north pole, theta equals zero, the qubit is all up. At the south pole, all down. The equator holds all equal superpositions.

build.suggested: suggested prompt: The two surviving parameters become a polar angle theta and an azimuth phi. Theta controls how much up versus how much down — at the north pole, theta equals ze

beats.narration_text: The Born-rule probability becomes clean geometry. Place the state arrow and the analyzer arrow on the sphere. Let gamma be the angle between them. The probability of 'up' is cosine-squared of half that angle. Perpendicular arrows give fifty percent. Parallel gives one hundred. Antiparallel gives zero.

build.suggested: suggested prompt: The Born-rule probability becomes clean geometry. Place the state arrow and the analyzer arrow on the sphere. Let gamma be the angle between them. The probabili

beats.narration_text: A physicist prepares a qubit at theta equals sixty degrees — two-thirds of the way from the north pole. She measures along the z-axis. The angle gamma equals sixty degrees. Probability of up equals cosine-squared of thirty degrees, which is three-quarters. She runs a thousand copies and gets about 750 up results.
beats.example_note: illustrative — qubit at theta=60 degrees, 1000 measurement trials

build.suggested: suggested prompt: A physicist prepares a qubit at theta equals sixty degrees — two-thirds of the way from the north pole. She measures along the z-axis. The angle gamma equals si

beats.narration_text: Why does every qubit live on a globe? Two complex amplitudes need four real parameters. Normalization removes one. Global phase removes another. Two remain — exactly two angles on a sphere. The Born-rule probability is just the cosine-squared of half the angle between state and analyzer.

card.copy: 4 real params - normalization - global phase = 2 = sphere. P(up) = cos^2(gamma/2).
